steps.title: Identify the sentence structure
steps.content: The sentence contains two independent clauses joined at the blank. Clause 1: "underlines, scribbles, and notes left in the margins by a former owner lower a book's [blank]." Clause 2 (after the dependent "when" phrase): "such markings...can be a gold mine to literary scholars." These two clauses contrast each other — one says markings lower value, the other says they can be valuable. Joining two contrasting independent clauses requires a comma plus a coordinating conjunction.

steps.title: Eliminate each wrong choice, then confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'value, but': A comma followed by the coordinating conjunction "but" correctly joins two contrasting independent clauses — this is the standard compound-sentence construction. ✓
  • Choice B — 'value': No punctuation or conjunction between the clauses creates a run-on sentence — two independent clauses cannot be fused without a connector. ✗
  • Choice C — 'value,': A comma alone between two independent clauses creates a comma splice — a coordinating conjunction is also required. ✗
  • Choice D — 'value but': The coordinating conjunction "but" is present but the required comma before it is missing — the comma must precede the conjunction when joining independent clauses. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
